slides.title: Train one leg at a time to *stay steady* for life.
slides.sub: Single-leg work evens out side-to-side strength and sharpens the reflexes that keep you on your feet.

slides.title: Most lifts hide a weak side
slides.body: Two-leg moves let your stronger leg carry the load. Over time that *quiet imbalance* grows without you noticing it.

slides.title: Each leg earns its own strength
slides.body: Split squats, step-ups and single-leg work *force both sides* to do equal effort, closing the gap between them.

slides.title: Balance is a trainable reflex
slides.body: Standing on one leg challenges the small stabilizers around your hips and ankles, the muscles that *catch a stumble* before it becomes a fall.

slides.title: Falls are a real risk later
slides.body: Lower-body strength and balance are linked to staying independent with age. Better stabilizers *may help* you avoid a serious fall.

slides.title: Add it to leg day
slides.body: Begin with a few sets of split squats or step-ups each week. Hold a wall for balance, then *let go as you steady*.

slides.title: Build a body that *stays balanced*.
slides.body: Vibe Health tracks your movement, steps, food and sleep, then turns the whole picture into one daily Health Score so progress stays clear.
slides.cta_text: search ‘Vibe Health’ on the App Store

caption:
Strong legs keep you upright, but a strong single leg keeps you steady. 🦵🌿

Unilateral work evens out your weaker side and sharpens the stabilizer reflexes that help prevent falls as you age. Save this for your next leg day. 🔖

Vibe Health turns your movement, sleep and food into one daily score → link in bio.
youtube_title: Why Single-Leg Training Keeps You Steady for Life
